column1: objectid004197,"James Metcalf to Ann Gill, Maccan River, Nova Scotia, August 1772.",record,4257,"James Metcalf to Ann Gill, Maccan River, Nova Scotia, August 1772.","Metcalf, James",In:,"DeWolfe, Barbara",Discoveries of America,,Cambridge,,"1772,  1997",pp.44-47,Full Text
August, 1772

My Dear: This comes to let you know that I am in good helth as these Lines I hope I shall find you, wee are meany Leagues part but Distance or lenth of time since we parted hath not made mee to forgit you, I have got 207 acers of land 33 acers of clear land very good land a good part of it will bee easly cleared, because it hath been formerly cut by the French, I and other two have 45 acers more for 5 years, and orchard that grows plenty of appels we desire to plow ye 45 acers and to sow it with wheat and other grane it is a pleasant and will be a frutefull place with cultivation  I need not say much of my place nor of the countery by this letter for I have described it in the other letter to my master only one thing I would tell you and that is a little flye caled a misketo that is troublesome in somer time and bites like a midge but I am told by the people that came to the place 8 or 9 years since that there is becom much fewer of them it is oweing to ye want of inhabitance and cattel to eat up the gras this is the only thing I have to say against the Country and now I put you to your promis that you promisd mee saying I will surely come to you and my Dear I shall be very glad to see you fulfill your promise to mee and I will fulfill mine to you if you come I will be a kind Husband to you and take you before aney other for I must marry for I cannot live well as I am, and as to your passage you need not bee affraide nor to let your thoughts to trouble you or to think how shall I undertake such a journey only try come and be not affraid I sopose that you will have plenty from Yorkshire to acompaney you O would I wear in the place of these lines and that I might be your companion but that must not be I have great besiness to do and cattle to look after so I cannot I can only pray to our God to protect and be your soport and guide when I was at sea I was sick but 2 half days half a day ye day that we imbarked and again sometime after when the sea was very Ruff and we all had a very good passage and were very helthfull. The people here are of different persusaions in religion they are mostly prisbyterians and Baptists ye church of England are fewer that either I believe that if one of our methodist preachers wear here he would be gladly received by people of all persusaions they are very strict in regard to ye Lords day and consious of family dutys but as to the mane thing in religion would it were more known among all people I trust that religion in its purity will be preached here also people here are naturally kind one to another even the Indians when a countryman comes to their wigwams are if they have aney meat at all they give him some.  Spinning wheels are very dear here for they are twenty shilings a peece English money pay for more then in England Ye Guney pays for thre and twenty and fower pence but all ye money in ye place is not English, there is a dollar that is 5s the pisterence that goes for a shiling every countrys money goes if peopel know its worth, all linen cloth and woolen cloth is very dear hear but they almost all grow thir own line and dres it themselves and the French and New England peopel, the women are mostly weavers and work their own both linen and wolen if you come pray be so good as to bring about a bushel of wheat if you can of 4 different kinds for seed let yellow Kent be one and Hampshire brown another for it will be of great servis hear be carefull to keep it from salt water you may if you please lay it like a pillow in your bed or in aney place where ye salt water does not come, provide a little tea or something that is nourishing provided you should be sea sick, I should be glad to see my master Wilkison hear but altho ye countrys good I would not advise him to come lest things should not do well so I might be blamed but he should I think he might do well hear, is nothing but the misketoes that is troublesome and they are bad to that they make a smoke at ye door sometimes in the evening to keep them out of their houses they are more troublesome then may imagin  but as I said it is for want of the Gras being mowed or eaten or burnt. This is ye only thing that I have to say against the place all things I things I think will be made up when inhabitance comes and trade increases if you come be not discoriged by aney thing in ye country for it is good if you come you will sail up to Fort Cumberland and when you are there write a line or two to me and send it to me to Maccan River by aney man and I will pay him and come for you but as soon as you receive my letter let me know your mind by letter and I will be as good as my word, the passage is paid at Liverpool before you go on bord but if you should not be abel to pay make friends to some that come and I will pay write to James Shanks at Liverpool about it. I must conclude for this time may ye Lord bles you and conduct you safe hither from
James Metcalf
If you write to mee you must direct to me at Maccan near Fort Cumberland to ye care of Govener Franklin at Halifax Nova Scotia. (directed to Miss.)
Mrs. Ann Gill With Mr. Thomas Wilkinson Martin Lordship near Ganongwould in Yorkshire, England.",,,"Metcalf, James

column3: ,"Ft: Excerpt..........................................................................................................................Thursday, 4 July 1844. Strong breezes NW and heavy squalls with rain. Cloudy Morning. PM fine with less wind. In the morning sent onshore to hire and have a waggon prepared for us, and at 11 left the Vessel accompanied by Doctor Kelly, on our way to Aux Lac {[Au Lac] in the Bay of Fundy, to wait upon Captain W.F.W Owen in HMSSS Coumbia, and to bring over chronometers which Captain Owen had for me. The heavy wind & rain & the distance to walk to the Waggon delayed us so that we could not start till noon. Crossed the great Tantamar [Tantramar] Marsh, or meadow more properly, an immense alluvial deposit covering 50 square miles or thereabouts, and as much more if the Amherst Marsh and other branches be taken into account. It appears to be entirely deposited by the high tides of the Bay of Fundy. We crossed the Tantamar, Auxlac & Westcot {Westcock}Rivers, and passed through the villages of Tantamar [Tantramar], Sackville and Westcot. Sackville a large place with several excellent buildings, also a church & several other  places of worship, & a large college belonging to the Wesleyan Methodists. The country everywhere diversified and beautiful. Especially about Sackville. ............Slept at the farm house of Mr. Snordon [Snowdon], opposite the Columbia lying off Shadwood Point.  [Wood Point]",,,"Sackville

column1: objectid004616,Sackville News: No Cases of Smallpox - At Mount Allison - The Methodist Church - Personal,record,4680,Sackville News: No Cases of Smallpox - At Mount Allison - The Methodist Church - Personal,"Smith, Alfred Dean",,,Saint John Globe,,,,"January 8, 1901",,Full Text
column3: ,"FT Excerpt:   No case of smallpox  has yet made its appearance in Sackville, nor has the disease drawn any nearer to us. Every day or two it is reported that  there is a fresh case in the infected district, but the disorder has so far continued to be of an entirely innocuous nature – no one dying and no one being seriously ill. If the quarantine  regulations can successfully be be enforced it will be probably confined to the region where it is at present prevalent. Dr. Calkin, health officer, has issued a notice requiring all unvaccinated persons to present themselves for vaccination within ten days , and stating that in case of failure to do so these persons will be visited by a doctor at their own expense.",,,"Smallpox

column1: objectid004617,"Sackville News: At the Mount Allison Institutions - Industrial, Political and Social Matters",record,4681,"Sackville News: At the Mount Allison Institutions - Industrial, Political and Social Matters","Smith, Alfred Dean",,,Saint John Globe,,,,"January 22, 1901",,Microform
column3: ,"FT Excerpt:      Last Wednesday our South African hero Will McMullen, clad in khaki, with rifle across his shoulders, pistols in his belt and sword at his side, gave an address to a crowded house  in the Methodist Church at Upper Sackville. Hitherto this brave fellow has received at the hands of the citizens of Sackville no public acknowledgement of his services. column1: objectid004621,Sackville News: Holidays at Mt Allison - Obit- -Manufacturing - Personal,record,4686,Sackville News: Holidays at Mt Allison - Obit- -Manufacturing - Personal,"Smith, Alfred Deane",,,Saint John Globe,,,,"April 8, 1901.",,Microform

Ft Excerpt:  Two other companies are shortly to be organized. One with capital of $24,0000 is being formed for the purpose of running the quarry opened by Charles Pickard three years ago. The company will probably run a short branch line from the quarry to the PEI railway thus securing easy access to the ICR, and by another branch of the latter being able to reach the wharf, will thus require exceptional facilities for shipment. The other proposed company is to be evolved from the existing firm of T.L. Black & Sons, and will be called the Joseph L. Black Co. Ltd., having for its object the carrying on of all the lumbering, milling, mercantile and agricultural business at present conducted by the above mentioned firm.  As an offset to the birth of this new corporation may be recorded the passing of the Sackville Machine Shop Co., which finally ceased and determined by the recent sale at auction of  its plant and premises. These were knocked down to W.C. Milner for $2200. With the draining of the Missiquash Marsh and other vast projects on his hands, Mr. Milner feels he can not afford the time to do justice to the running of a machine shop, and so has has sold the machinery en bloc to Mr. Peters of Moncton, retaining the shop and land to serve doubtless  as the basis of a grand real estate coup at some date in the future. Mr. Milner, by the way, reports himself thoroughly confident of the eventual success of his Missiquash marsh draining operations. Quite a number of canals and ditches, however have yet to be cut before the reclamation of the entire marsh  - about 6000 acres – shall be complete, and these he proposes to take in hand as soon as the season is sufficiently advanced.",,,"Pugsley, Dr.

FT Except:  Important repairs and improvements are being effected by the Standard Manufacturing Co.  The Enterprise Foundry Co. is putting up a new fitting shop and may before the end of the season build a new moulding shop and put in a new blast furnace. Silas W. Copp. who a month ago bought the old central school house, is converting it into a double tenement house. Messrs. H. & H. Copp have the new lockup substantially completed and the question now is who shall be its first occupant.  . . .. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .. . . . . . . . .  .Work has begun on the new road from Squire Street to the Harris aboideau. This road will go directly across the Marsh and will furnish a straight and level means of access to Middle Sackville – a pleasant substitute  for the curves and hills of the present road.  . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . ....The greatest improvement however, for the present season is to be found in the projected water works. The company for the carrying out of this scheme has been fully organized under the presidency of Senator Wood  and W.C. Milner as Secretary and Thomas Murray as Treasurer. The stock list is now going round and it is thought that no difficulty will be found in getting the $45,000 capital subscribed. The source of the water is a brook, fed by a number of splendid springs, some distance back of Ogden’s mill pond and about two hundred and seventy feet above the ground at Crane’s Corners. Whatever may be the success of this project as a means of supplying water for family and manufacturing purposes its importance as an auxiliary in fighting fires can not be over estimated. It certainly marks the greatest single advance to be found in the history of Sackville.",,,"Borden, Byron Crane 1850-1929

column1: objectid004626,Sackville News: Affairs at Mount Allison Institutions - Stone Export Trade Ceased - Personal - An Old Bridge,record,4691,Sackville News: Affairs at Mount Allison Institutions - Stone Export Trade Ceased - Personal - An Old Bridge,"Smith, Alfred Deane",,,Saint John Globe,,,,"June 24, 1901",,Full Text
column3: ,"FT Excerpt:    The export of stone from the Woodpoint quarries to the United States, which for  number of years bought A good deal of money into this part of the province, has now entirely ceased. Last week the engines and plant of all kinds belonging to one of these quarries, which had formerly been operated by a New York Company, were sold at auction. The quarry adjoining this one was the first to be opened, commencing operations somewhere about 1868 by supplying the Intercolonial with stone for its bridges, culverst etc. The quarry was subsequently developed at great expense by Austin and Walter Roberts, of Boston and was sold by them to Bedford Read, who parted with it to William Clarke of Woodpoint. Mr. Clarke, after making a handsome competency out of it  sold it in turn to H.C. Read, brother of Bedford. This summer Mr. Read  is supplying from it the stone for the pier at Cape Tormentine. The Pickard quarry in Sackville, in addition to the in addition to the three contracts previously mentioned , is to supply the stone for the new building of the people’s bank in Fredericton and also for a large building in St. John. . . . .  .  .  .  .   .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .    .    .    .    .     .     .   .   .   .   .   .  . .. ..  In his recent St. John address, Prof. Duff of UNB, when discussing and comparing steam and water power their comparative costs etc.,  had something to say of tide mills.  It may not be uninteresting to mention the fact that Sackville, some eighty years ago, had a tide mill. It was situated on the creek spanned by the aboideau on the road to Woodpoint, and stood some little distance back from the road on the right hand side going down.  It is safe to say that if tide mills should ever become the fashion, Sackville, with the high tides rushing up the Tantramar and its various tributaries and creeks, could really beat the world.  . .   .   .    .    .    .   . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .  .  .   . .  .  .   .  . .   . .    . .   . . . .. . . .... . .  Another Sackville antiquity still in existence is to be found in the old map of the Parish of Sackville, owned by Thomas Estabrooks of the Brunswick House , and purchased a number of years ago by his father  at the sale of Judge Botsford’s effects. This map dates back to 1804 and is a certified copy of a map made by surveyor general Sproule. It shows the homestead lots as granted by the crown in 1760 and 1761 to the original settlers from the United States, and still continues to be of great utility in determining disputed boundary line etc. etc.",,,"Borden, Byron Crane

column1: objectid004692,Valuable Real Estate for Sale,record,4757,Valuable Real Estate for Sale,,,,Borderer and Westmorland and Cumberland Advertiser,,,,"November 17, 1876",p.3,Microform
column3: |Full Text,"FT Excerpt:“Valuable Real Estate For Sale! The Subscriber will sell at private sale that valuable and pleasantly situated homestead where he now resides, with all buildings thereon. Only five minutes walk from the Railway Station, Telegraph and Post Offices or will sell the same in small lots to suit purchasers, for building purposes, from a quarter of an acre up. The land is in a high state of cultivation, with garden and orchard. There is a road all around the premises, and choice building lots can be selected on every part of the farm. Also his mill, 175 feet long, 40 feet wide with all the gear in it. Also his well known shipyard, shops, sheds, store and dwelling house. There is on this property a New Wharf where vessels can sail out of the river without tacking. The Yard, including Mill site, contains eight acres. Also, adjoining this property, will sell twenty-two acres of prime English marsh, in good order. Also that well known and desirable property known as the Jonathan C. Black, place, so called, the whole or in lots to suit purchasers. Also two lots of woodland, on the Church Road, at Westcock, near Thomas Milner’s. Also, one lot near Johnson’s Mill, Grand Aunce, and three hundred acres of well timbered Land on Second Westcock Hill, near the English Church. Also, the Brigantine “GEM.” This vessel is new, was classed and coppered this present year, is a fast sailer and carries five hundred Tons, dead weight, has iron water tanks and a full and complete East India, and African outfit. Also a vessel’s frame in the ship [text cut out] and fixtures complete. Good warranted unencumbered titles given to all the above property. For further particulars apply to the Subscriber. C. Boultenhouse. ”",,,"Boultenhouse, Christopher 1802-1876

column1: objectid004693,Valuable Real Estate for Sale,record,4758,Valuable Real Estate for Sale,,,,Borderer and Westmorland and Cumberland Advertiser,,,,"May 3, 1877",p.3,Advertisement
column4: ,"“Valuable Real Estate For Sale! The subscribers will sell at private sale that valuable and pleasantly situated property in Sackville called the C. Boultenhouse Estate Property, only a few minutes walk from the Railroad Station, Telegraph and Post Offices. The Land is in a high state of cultivation and will be sold in lots to suit purchasers from one quarter of an acre up. Also, that valuable and pleasantly situated property called the Jonathan C. Black property Also 22 acres of Prime English Marsh Land. Also, the well known ShipYard with Shops, Sheds and Dwelling House thereon, and a wharf where vessels can lay at or sail from. Also a Wood Lot, near Thomas Milner’s on the road leading from West cock Church to Grand Aunce. If the above property is not disposed of before the 22nd day of May nest, will then be offered at Public Auction on that day, sale commencing at 10 o’clock a.m. Good warranted titles given to all the above property. For further particulars enquire of the Subscribers. Arabella Boultenhouse Executrix, John Ford, Executor. Sackville March 18th 1877.”",,,"Boultenhouse, Christopher 1802-1876

column1: objectid004694,Obituary,record,4759,Obituary,,,,Borderer and Westmorland and Cumberland Advertiser,,,,"August 12, 1875",p.2,Microform
column3: ,"FT: “OBITUARY. Our readers will notice with regret the announcement of the death of Mariner Wood, Esq. For many years Mr. Wood’s business standing and personal character have made him one of the most prominent members of the community. Into the retirement to which failing health some year or two since compelled him to withdraw, he took with him the respect and sympathy of all classes. The steps by which his extensive business and fortune were built up were sure and well considered, and his name stood at the highest point of commercial trustworthiness. The religious and educational interests with which he was identified and which he generously supported will greatly miss him.”",,,"Wood, Mariner      - 1875

column1: objectid004701,Our Workshops and Manufactories: The Carriage Shops,record,4766,Our Workshops and Manufactories: The Carriage Shops,,,,Borderer and Westmorland and Cumberland Advertiser,,,,"June 16, 1870",p.2,Microform
column3: ,"FT: “OUR WORKSHOPS AND MANUFACTORIES. The Carriage Shops.                             CAMPBELL’S CARRIAGE FACTORY.

column1: This establishment is in Upper Sackville, in the vicinity of Morice’s Mills. A large building, 70 by 30 feet, contains, on the ground floor, the workshop in the end fronting the road, the machine shop in the middle of the building, and in the rear end the horse-power for driving the machinery. Up-stairs are the store room, paint and varnishing rooms. The building and premises were purchased of Mr. John Beal who built and used it several years as a tannery. There is also a commodious blacksmith’s shop on the premises. Four journeymen carriage makers and two blacksmiths are constantly employed
column2: there are also three apprentices. The workshops are furnished with hand tools of every description. The horse-power is set in motion by one or more horses, according to the kind of work. Beside the circular, jigsaws and lathes which it drives, are an emery belt for smoothing spokes, and a heavy grindstone for grinding the steel springs made in the blacksmith shop.............................................................................................................

column1: From 30 to 40 wheeled vehicles and about 20 sleighs and pungs are annually turned out of this establishment. A large amount of repairing is also done. The proprietor superintends the working operations himself, and manages the requisite business transactions. The nucleus of this establishment was begun by Mr. C’s father, the late Mr. Ronald Campbell, and is of over 20 years standing. Keeping pace with the growth of the country, we predict for it a long and prosperous career. Mr. Campbell is a Justice of the Peace. ...................................................................................................................                                                                                                                                                                   MR. DAVID EASTERBROOKS’

establishment, also in Upper Sackville, consists of a blacksmith’s shop and a two story building, 30 by 26 feet, containing their usual working apartments, with their customary apperatus, of a carriage maker’s shop. He keeps a blacksmith constantly employed, and two or three carriage makers. The kind of work done in other carriage shops is done here. His sawing, turning and boring are at present done at Mitchell’s Mill, Beech Hill, but he intends soon to have a horse-power. Though he has been in business but a few years, and with but little capital to start with, Mr. E has succeeded in founding an establishment which bids fair to grow to larger proportions. It already turns out, yearly, about 30 vehicles of all kinds, besides repairing........................................................................................................
WHEATON’S CARRIAGE FACTORY.

This widely known stand has a history of 40 years. It was founded by the later Mr. Calvin Wheaton, afterwards continued under the name of Wheaton Bros., and is now in the hands of Mr. Daniel Wheaton, one of the late firm. It employs four men and turns out from 30 to 40 wheeled vehicles, besides a number of sleighs and pungs. Most of the steel springs used in this establishment are manufactured on the premises, and preparations are being made to make axles also. It consists of a number of buildings united together, erected at different periods as they were required. The horse-power occupies the lower part of a lofty two story building, 30 by 30, and is driven by one or four horses, as may be needed. The machine and workshops are contiguous. Circular and jig saws, lathes, drills, &c., perform the requisite multiplied operations. The paint room up stairs is the largest and loftiest of its kind in Sackville. Mr. W’s carriages are extensively circulated. One sent to Barbadoes some years since found a ready sale. He is now filling an order filling an order from Newcastle for three............................................................................................................
MR. MERRITT WHEATON’S

shop in the same neighborhood, is a spacious two story building. We neglected to get its dimensions while visiting it, but would judge it to be about 26 by 40. It contains and has ample room for all the apartments of a carriage shop, fitted up with every requisite appurtenance. He employs two or three men, and yearly disposes of from 20 to 25 waggons of all kinds, besides sleighs and pungs. Mr. W. is a son of the later Mr. Calvin Wheaton who started the first carriage factory of note in Sackville. He is a young man, and promises by his mechanical skill and close application to business to make his establishment take a number one rank among the Sackville Manufactories.

The iron work for this shop is done by the proprietor’s uncle, Mr. Elisha Wheaton, whose Blacksmith Shop was not noticed under that head, as he is so considerable engaged, in connection with his nephew, in the manufacture of carriages. There are three men employed in this shop, and three forges in almost constant blast. It contains a machine, the same as the one already noticed belonging to Mr. Bowser, for cutting, punching and “upsetting,” which has, in addition, attached to it, a contrivance for bending tires. Beside wagon ironing, the general custom of the country is largely attended to. Mr. Wheaton is a blacksmith of long standing, and was a member of the late firm of Wheaton Brothers........................................................................................................

In this neighborhood there is also another Blacksmith’s Shop belonging to Mr. James R. King, which should have been mentioned under the head of Blacksmiths. Mr. King was absent when we visited his shop, but we are warranted in saying that he efficiently meets a fair share of custom work, as well as irons a number of carriages yearly. The shop was formerly occupied by Mr. James Fillmore who has removed to California.
The last named four establishments, in such close proximity, keep up an activity highly pleasing and salutary to the neighborhood.
Mr. M. Hicks’ Carriage Shop on the Beech Hill road, the last in the catalogue, will commence our next article.”",,,"Carriage Factories

column1: objectid004708,Sackville News: The Oldest Industrial Site in the Province - Quarrying - Gaspereaux Plentiful,record,4773,Sackville News: The Oldest Industrial Site in the Province - Quarrying - Gaspereaux Plentiful,"Smith, Alfred Deane",,,Saint John Globe,,,,"May 13, 1907",,Microform
column3: ,"FT: EXCERPT "" Now at a meeting of the Committee for the Township of Sackville in Nova Scotia, held on the 20th of July, 1792 at the inn of Mrs. Charity Bishop, Cumberland - the first public meeting probably held in the province - it was resolved in connection with the division of the land among intending settlers that the lower Mill Creek be the dividing line between Westcock Village and the MIddle Village and that the other boundry of the middle Village be at the old mill dam at the Upper Mill Creek. This mill dam, which on the site indicated above was evidently a French structure and very likely dated back to the seventeenth century.For how much of the time which has elapsed since the date mentioned above there has been a mill in operation on this site is not known - probably however, for most of the 145 years. if not all of them.i",,,"Middle Sackville

column1: objectid004713,Sackville News: Sackville Water Supply Must be increased - The Weather and the Crops - Good Exhibition - Property Changes Hands - Personal,record,4778,Sackville News: Sackville Water Supply Must be increased - The Weather and the Crops - Good Exhibition - Property Changes Hands - Personal,"Smith, Alfred Deane",,,Saint John Globe,,,,"October 15, 1906.",,Microform
column3: ,"FT: Excerpt  ""The exhibition of the Sackville and Westmorland Agricultural Society opened on Thursday morning and closed on Friday evening. Owing to the bad weather the attendance on Thursday was small, the sale of the 25 cent tickets footing up to only $62, But Friday's sunshine brought the people out in swarms.

column1: The exhibition was an exceptionally good one. The show of horses was excellent, that of horned cattle still better - one of the best indeed, ever seen on the grounds - and the poultry exhibit was a very attractive one.Surprisingly good too, was the display of vegetables especially in view of the dry summer, while the beets,mangolds and carrots were nothing , the turnips, cabbage, cauliflower and potatoes were immense. On Friday afternoon there was something going on around the track, which was supposed to be racing, but the more expert wholly failed to recognize it as such............. . . . . ..

One of the oldest houses in Sackville recently changed hands This was the cottage in the vicinity of the old Presbyterian church, immediatley to the north of Dr. Andrew's property, built about 90 years ago by Dr, Marmaduke Blackhouse, one of Sackville's early physicians, and afterwards for many years registrar of wills  and deeds at Dorchester. This has been bought from the estate of the late Ainsley Bowser by Charles Sears, of Midgic. On the lot of land on the north side of Bridge Street for thirty years or more the site of of the office of the Post, Miss Ella Read, late of Amherst, contemplates building a two-storey apartment house, 65 feet by 35 feet, the upper flat of which she will herself occupy. John Fillmore, who is the contractor, has the cellar excavated and the cellar  wall well under way.""",,,"Reservoirs

column1: No Tribune made its appearance on Monday, but on Thursday was issued the first number to be printed in Editor Avard’s splendid new brick building on Main Street. This office is certainly the finests one of the kind in the Maritime Provinces outsida of Saint John and Halifax – well built and fin ished, well lighted and commodious-and if the spirit back of all these improvements is projected into the management of the paper what a journal may not the Tribune become.”",,,"Borden, Byron Crane

column1: objectid004722,Sackville News: Prospective Diplomatists - Dr. Archibald Leaving the Staff of the Ladies College,record,4787,Sackville News: Prospective Diplomatists - Dr. Archibald Leaving the Staff of the Ladies College,"Smith, Alfred Deane",,,,Saint John Globe,,,"May 20, 1907",,Microform
column3: ,"FT:  Excerpt  “ . . . .    The Ladies College has met with another very serious loss in the resignation of Dr. R.C. Archibald, Mt.A 1894, professor of violin music. After graduating from the university in arts and in music at the Mt. Allison  Conservatory, Mr. Archibald spent three years at Harvard, where he took his M.A.in mathematics, prosecuting his violin music also under Charles N. Allan. He then took a two years’ course in mathematics at Strasburg Germany, winning the degree of Ph.D. and still carrying on his violin study under Boise, Butler and Schuster.While teaching at the Ladies College for a number of years past, not only has he proved himself a master, and an enthusiast in his special line of teaching, but has also confrerred on the institution inestimable benefits by his remarkable, nay, unique, gifts as librarian and bibliographer. Thanks mainly to him, the college now has a library of some 7000 volumes, all valuable and many rare, housed in a most comfortable and cheerful room, and in addition to this an apartment filled with magazines of every description, nearly all bound. To Dr. Archibald is also due the remarkable and complete system of card catalogues of student’s names that has been in progress of compilation for several years and has now, as far as the past is concerned, arrived at practical completion Dr. Archibald has not yet made up his mind whether he will take a chair of mathematics or go abroad for additional mathematical study. He expects, however, to remain in Sackville until next autumn.”",,,"Degrees

column1: objectid004728,Sackville News: Swamp Reclaimed – Splendid Racing Plant Established – Personal,record,4794,Sackville News: Swamp Reclaimed – Splendid Racing Plant Established – Personal,"Smith, Alfred Deane",,,Saint John Globe,,,,"July 22, 1907",,Microform
column3: ,"FT: Excerpt “....By extensive draining operations combined with the tidal deposit of fertilizing mud the vast Missiquash Marsh, lying to the eastward of Point de Bute ridge, is being evolved from what was not long ago an impassable and useless swamp. For five miles up from Mount Whatley the Missiquash River has been converted by the steam dredge into an excellent canal and for three miles of the five, a lateral system of canals and ditches has been excavated. To reach the end of the marsh the river will have to be dredged for a couple of miles further and, when this is done, there will be four miles of the main canal to be supplemented by the same subsidiary drainage system which is in operation along the lower three miles of the canal. It will be seen therefor, that, while much has been done in connection with this great work of reclamation, much yet remains to be done. Last year the total revenues of the marsh, derived from pasturage fees and hay sold, sufficed to pay running expenses, and to clear for the proprietors a dividend of two percent. Owing to extensive winter kill the yield of grass this season may not be so much in advance of last season’s cut, as it actually should be, but the excellent growing weather which has prevailed for the lst three weeks is daily rendering the outlook more encouraging.

column1: Down at the Agricultural Society’s grounds the new half mile race track is well on its way to completion, and the contractor Charles Mitten has begun the work of putting up the grand stand, judge’s stand, etc. etc.",,,"Marsh Reclamation

The persons in Sackville – aldermen and others – who have been discussing the wharf problem and have been devising schemes for the municiple ownership of the wharves down at the Landing and for getting the government to repair and remodel the same, have not considered what has for some time been going on in the Tantramar River.  The tidal action is rapidly wearing away the isthmus which to the bank of the river the peninsula  known as the Ram Pasture, which is directly across the stream from the place where the wharves are situated. When the river finds vent through this isthmus and deserts its present circuitous channel round the peninsula aforesaid, this channel will inevitably  fill up and the wharves, owing to the deposit of the tidal mud, will eventually be put out of commission. In that event new wharves would have to be built further up stream probably in the vicinity of the covered bridge so as to be accessible by a highway. A number of years ago, when a similar catastrophe appeared to be pending, the government fortified the seaward side of the neck or isthmus by driving in a number of piles and thus stayed the catastrophe.",,,"Fawcett, Albert T.

Our Town Council Purposes to extend our water system from the vicinity of J.L. Black and Sons quite a distance along towards Upper Sackville so as to supply Councillor Clinton Campbell and Will Cambell and others. Last summer the reservoir all but went dry and since then a number of services has been put in within the town limits, so that the wisdom of this projected extension is open to very great doubt.

Charles Pickard and Charles Fawcett Jr. proprietors of the Sackville Electric Light Company, have begun the contemplated improvements in their plant. They are putting up the posts for running their wires down to the covered Bridge which will in furture be lighted at nights. They are also having the town completely rewired and will put on a separate wire the street lights of the town, as they do not need to be lighted so early as those stores and houses. And as they now propose to run run an all night system the new ICR stataion will be equipped with the electric light, very much to the convenience of the officials and the public at large.",,,"Bond, George J.

column1: objectid004754,Sackville News: Class in Surveying Busy – Improving in Health – New Road Asked For,record,4822,Sackville News: Class in Surveying Busy – Improving in Health – New Road Asked For,"Smith, Alfred Deane",,,Saint John  Globe,,,,"October 28, 1907",,Full Text
column3: ,"Ft Exerpt:       Having in the last two years surveyed and mapped in two section the property of the Institutions lying on both sides of York Street and main Street, Professor Crowell’s class in surveying will this autumn take in hand the portion of York Street Stretching to the westward of Salem Street. This region is semi-institutional in its nature, containing as it does the residences of four of the University professors and , therefore, lends itself naturally to the manipulation of University students.  Professor Sweetser reports himself from Boston to Professor Crowell as somewhat improving in Health and hopeful of being able to undertake some light professorial work in the nor very remote future. Dr. Andrews is a t present is engaged in making an exhaustive examination of the merits and defects of the various actylene  gas plants offered for sale. The system selected will be set up in the basement story of the Science Building under the stairs and the gas will probably be used not only for experimental purposes at the student’s desk in the chemistry room , but also to a large extent for lighting the buildings. At the student’s desks it will be consumed in the Bunsen burner , the hear thus generated being of extraordinary intensity,high enough in fact to fuse almost anything. The gas and water pipes in the chemistry rooms up stairs are now all laid and very great precautions have been taken to exclude by the use of sawdust, packing etc. the freezing air currents for which the old building has always been justly celebrated. Part of the flooring is down and Dr. Andrews is having it treated with “ Granite Varnish” which he reports as imparting to woodwork a hardness and solidity fairly adamantine.",,,"Colpitts, Elmer C.

column209: ,"FT: Excerpt    The weather we have had  in November has so far been the finest of the past six months and our farmers have been actively utilizing it in getting in their broadleaf hay which, though not of the highest quality will yet serve to keep through the winter the young cattle which are not yet connoisseurs of feed as they will be when they get older. A good deal of fall plowing and work on the marsh has also been done. A task in this line which might be attended to is the rebuilding and strengthening of the isthmus which joins to the bank of the river , the piece of the marsh known as the Ram Pasture, which , if nothing is done, will soon be an island.",,,"Hay

column245: ,"FT: Excerpt    Bill C. Rayworth, one of our brightest and most enterprising business men, who makes and sells carriages and vehicles of other kinds and deals in harness, fur coats etc. has developed a growing trade in wheel hubs. These he sells all over the Maritimes. He is also corresponding with a large English firm and will probably soon be  making trans Atlantic shipments of Sackville hubs. Mr. Rayworth is commencing his winter operations in this line by advertising for yellow birch timber for 20,000 hubs.",,,"Eurhetorian Society

column395: ,"FT: Excerpt     Several years ago, Blair Taylor bought, from the H.B. Allison estate a good sized lot on Main Street in the hollow to the southward of the house and erected close to the street a carriage-making establishment  and back on the hillside, a dwelling house. This property eventually  fell into the hands of George E. Ford, who, a couple of years ago, sold the house to his book-keeper, Mr. Williams and recently disposed of the carriage shop to the newly formed paper box company who expect to begin operations therein at an early date. The Directors of the company are J.H. Secord, A. B. Copp, M.P.P. S.W. Copp, Thos. R. Anderson,  and C.C. Avard, Dr. Secord being Pesident of the company and Mr. Avard Secretary. The last named gentleman was the originator of the whole scheme.",,,"Lathes

column443: objectid004785,Sackville News: New Engine Installed in Fawcett Foundry - Large Attendance at Academy - Trial Debate,record,4854,Sackville News: New Engine Installed in Fawcett Foundry - Large Attendance at Academy - Trial Debate,Smith, Alfred Deane",,,Saint John Globe,,,,"January 13, 1908",,Microform
column445: ,"FT Excerpt:  In the brick power house erected last autumn by the Charles Fawcett Company the boiler and the fine new 175 horse power engine are set up and in running order. All that is lacking is the great twenty inch belt which has been made in Toronto, and was expected to arrive tomorrow. The fire in the boiler room was lighted for the first time yesterday morning and it is hoped that by the middle of next week the machinery will be under way and the moulders and fitters all at work. The new engine is equipped with all the modern devices which promote efficiency and economy. After the steam, for instance, has played its part  in the cylinder it will circulate through pipes about the shop as a heating agent and then, condensing into water, and having extracted from it the lubricating oil it has accumulated on the way, it will  pass through a feed-water-heater and thence again be automatically injected into the boiler at a temperature not much lower than boiling. What all this means in the saving of fuel and labour will be easily seen.  Probably no other foundry in the Maritime Provinces has now an equipment so thorough and up to date as this one to which reference is here made.  Charles Pickard expects, early in the spring to put a powerful new engine in the quarry power house. He has the floor now concreted, the boiler in place, the walls surrounded with heating pipes etc. etc.                                                                                                                       The two new dynamos for the electric light station have arrived and will be put in place in the course of next week. The committee of University professors, which was appointed  to consider the question of an electric light station for the institutions has been giving the question a great deal of attention and expects to have a careful and exhaustive report to lay before the Board of Regents at the annual meeting on the 27th of May",,,"Power House

column478: objectid004786,Sackville News:  Nugget Found in Crop of Duck - Mt. Allison Institutions,record,4855,Sackville News:  Nugget Found in Crop of Duck - Mt. Allison Institutions,Smith, Alfred Deane",,,Saint John Globe,,,,"January 21, 1908",,Microform
column480: ,"Ft Excerpt:   The committee of University professors having under consideration  the problem of electric lighting for the institutions at first contemplated on the erection at some central point of a power-house with steam engine and dynamos. But their attention eventually became drawn to the possibility of using water as a motive power , and they are now considering a couple of water privileges , not far from each other, in the vicinity of Calhoun’s Mills and on the same stream, about five miles to the northwest of Sackville. This week, K.C. Archibald, C.E. went carefully over the ground and expressed a favorable opinion as to the quantity of water that can be impounded at the two sites, the depth of the dam needed etc. etc.

column44: ,"FT Excerpt: At  the Science Building the new lathe is in successful operation. It is technically known as a “14 screw-cutting engine lathe” and is of the most modern design  and equipment. This machine including back rest, follow rest, compound rest, independent four jaw chuck, and all other accessories necessary for accurate work was purchased from  the Seneca Falls Manufacturing Company , New York State, and certainly reflects very great credit on its makers. A few test pieces have been turned, and the machine has been found to be well adjusted, accurate, stiff enough he stand the heaviest work and capable of cutting-without compounding-almost any thread from 3 to 64. . . .",,,"Sackville Electric Light and Telephone Company

column62: ,"FT Excerpt:  With the exception of one or two little items of plumbing the new Intercolonial Railway station is now completed and Contractor Anderson expects to have it taken off his hands in the course of next week. It is a well built, convenient one-story structure of stone, finished inside, with the exception of the baggage room, with different varieties of hardwood, heated by hot water, well supplied with water taps. Etc. and lighted by electricity.  .  .  .  .   .  .  .  .  .   .  .  .  .  .  .  DOWN AT WESTOCK a staff of engineers is engaged in taking borings in the vicinity of the old mill dam on the south side of the creek with the view of determining the most suitable place for the railway bridge which will have to be constructed in case the suggested piece of railway is constructed between Dorchester and Sackville. The route of the section as at present staked off  starts a little to the westward of Dorchester station and runs nearly south past Dorchester Cape to Grand Aunce. The it turns to the north of the creek and goes northeast across the peninsula, passing to the rear of British Settlement, between Wood point and Westcock, and along the edge of the upland as far as the old shipyard or thereabouts. This gives a line which, if it is true, is very far from being straight, but which, on the other hand, avoids the heavy grades on the existing section of railway, which is itself not a straight line.                                                                                                                                                                               THE NEW DYNAMOS in the electric light station have in the course of the last two nights been substituted for the old ones and the necessary connections have been made, though the change involves a good deal more work especially in reference to the transformers. The electric light will after this be supplied all night.  The negotiations that have been going on between the Sackville Electric light  Company and the Chignecto Power Company are said to have resulted in this, that the former concern has offered its plant, etc. to the latter at a certain price, the accepting or declining of which will settle the matter one way or another.",,,"Elections

column3: ,"Ft Excerpt:  The isthmus joining to the river bank the peninsular body of marsh known as the Ram Pasture has been at length warn through by the unceasing action of the tides and currents of the Tantramar and the peninsula has now become an island. This piece of marsh, which is situated between the railway bridge and the landing contains no less than 210 acres, and is owned by several proprietors, who are now making every effort to get their hay removed before the isthmus becomes absolutely impassable, a condition which can not be far from realization. They do not purpose to make any attempt at repairs, but intend, so it is rumored, to ask the local government to build a bridge for them to cross the chasm.",,,"Ram Pasture Marsh

column41: ,"FT Excerpt:    The freestone quarry at Woodpoint, which was opened some forty or fifty years ago, and has since that time been worked by different companies, is now being successfully operated by Henry C. Read, Further down the in the same direction Mr. Read has two other quarries, one at Squaw’s Cap and another at Slack’s Cove , and this spring he intends to open still another  at the last mentioned place . In connection with the latter quarry he will install a powerful engine and turning lathe to be employed in the manufacture of grindstones. Still further along to the southwest  there used to be years ago in the vicinity of cape Marenguin a quarry from which Peter  Hagau supplied the stone for the Saint John Custom House. At Grand Aunce, on the northern side of the peninsula , in  which are located the above mentioned places, the Albert Manufacturing Company is operating a plaster quarry from which were shipped last year, over 20,000 tons. In connection with the quarry there is a pier 100 foot long, which during the approaching season is to be extended 200 feet further, so as to obviate the necessity of  using scows in shipping the plaster.",,,"Gates, Reginald Ruggles

column191: ,"FT Excerpt:     The members of Professor Crowell’s class in surveying are now engaged in the task of putting into map form the results of their survey of last autumn. The scene of their operations was the section of land lying between Salem Street, York street, the western fence of the cemetery and southern fence of Fred Ryan’s property. It is interesting to note that Professor Crowell is conducting his annual surveys with the resultant maps in careful conformity with the work already done so that eventually, when he has covered all the ground occupied by the town of Sackville, he will be able easily to combine all his sectional maps into one complete and minutely accurate map of said town.",,,"Lowerison Robert

column1: Ft Excerpt:     B.C. Rayworth has not been able this season to to get nearly  as much yellow birch as he would like for the carriage hubs which h manufacture. He has discovered he can utilize his metal working lather in giving them their first rough turning and boring them, the work being done with far greater speed and efficiency than would be attainable by an ordinary carpenter’s lathe. After this first process the hubs are thoroughly boiled and then laid aside to season, being finished after they are ordered, as then they can be made of any required size or shape.",,,"Roads

column122: ,"Ft Excerpt: The government engineer who has been here considering the proper site for the projected wharf was urged by some whose opinion is of exceptional weight to have it located on or near the old Boltenhouse shipyard. This yard, where 50 or 60 years ago, Christopher Boultenhouse built many fine vessels lies on the right hand side of the river about half a mile from the existing wharves at the Landing and nearer the mouth of the Tabtramar. What particularly recommends this site is that its adoption would eliminate an awkward river curve between it and the Landing, so that navigation between the Bay and the wharf would be greatly facilitated.",,,"Wharves

column395: ,"Ft Excerpt:  Sackville imports very little by water and although, while navigation is open, there are almost always two or three vessesls lying at the wharves down at the landing, the freight in these is mainly for Rhodes and Curry of Amherst consisting of lumber of various Kinds, car wheels, Coal etc. Between this and Christmas, six or seven vessles consigned to this firm are expected to arrive. Early this season Captain Elijah Chase set up a steam engine on one of the wharves and  by its help hoisted out the cargos of the vessels and stowed them in the cars to be shipped by rail, thus finding steady and not unrenumerative employment. As nobody in Sackville is particulary interested in the wharves at the landing they are not kept in very good repair. In fact our port and all its surroundings are in a very unsatisfactory condition. There is a rumour that if there is not soon a change for the better the aformentioned firm will rent the Fort Lawrence dock, excavated by the Ship Railway Company, and fit it up with wharves etc. etc. for a harbor, leaving the Sackville landing to its own fate.  Two of our merchants this summer had quite a quarrel down there about a ship's birth, with the result that legal proceedings were taken by one the the parties in the fray,",,,"Wharves

column480: During the week Dr.  Borden has been trying experiments with his island fountain, but in the face of all hydraulic laws the water refused to rise higher than a couple of feet. Engineer Archibald, naturally resenting any imputations on the water, of which he is the official representative, puts the blame on the terminal arrangements of the fountain, and thinks that when these are rectified, we shall see the jet of water  ""shake its loosened silver in the sun"".",,,"Bulmer, Joseph

column558: ,"FT Excerpt. column379: ,"FT Excerpt:  The most interesting event of recent date in the business world of Sackville is the change by letters patent of the firm of Joseph L. Black and Son  into a company of five stockholders limited, these stockholders being Joseph L. Black, Frank B. Black, John W.S. Black, Jennie Black and Burton Black. This business was started 49 years ago by Joseph L. Black, who had come over from Amherst two years previously as clerk to his uncle Samuel, who had been doing business not far from Mr. Black's present stand, but had been burned out. Mr. Black commenced operations in a small building on the south side of the road, but in a few years erected on the oposite side his present store, at that time incomparably the finest one in Sackville, and even yet, scarcely surpassed in any way. It is very generally said and believed that Mr. Black is the hardest working man in Sackville. If so, his work has thoroughly agreed with him, for though he has passes by two or three years, the three score years and ten limit, yet is his eye "" not dim nor his natural force abated."" It would indeed be hard to find a straighter, fresher looking, more able-bodied man or a man of more diversified business activites, keeping in his store pretty nearly everything that any can reqiure, manufacturing lumber on a large scale, and conducting on of the largest and finest farms in the county - a farm which cuts upwards of 500 tons of hay, and much of which has been reclaimed by himself. Mr. Black is a grand-nephew of William Black, the apostle of Methodism, and brother-in-law of Governor Snowball.",,,"Snowball, Jabez Bunting 1837-1907

column1287: ,"FT Excerpt:  The wave  of commercial and industrial amalgamation which has been sweeping over the continent has reached Sackville and our two water companies, have ""like kindred drops been mingled into one"". Some fifteen years ago the late Thomas Pickard formed a little company to utilize a fine spring in the rear of his residence on York Street in supplying a limited portion of the community with water. The whole plant consisted of the spring, a pump, a windmill, a little steam engine and a large tank on higher ground which was kept filled by the opertions of the mill reinforced by the engine in a season of calm. Eventually this concern supplied the University Residence, the Ladies College and six or seven private houses with what is allowed to be very superior water. But there were considerable bother and expense connected with its operation and this circumstance coupled with others which need not here be detailed, led to negotiations with the new Sackville Water Company, which have resulted in a combination of the two on terms not yet given to the public. This combination really means the extinction of the smaller company, as it is not probable that the other company will continue to operate its plant. The lion and lamb have lain down together, but the lamb is inside.",,,"Pickard, Thomas

column21: ,"FT Excerpt:     “Obituary: Mr. Thomas D. Vickery, of the late firm of Lindsay & Vickery, died on Sunday evening last, after an illness of six weeks. He was a native of London, England, and emigrated to St. John some years since, where he was employed in the London House until he commenced business in Sackville, in 1855. He was an energetic and enterprising business man, and evinced considerable interest in agricultural matters. He will be greatly missed in this community, where he leaves numerous friends. – The remains were taken to St. John on Tuesday morning by train and were interred there that afternoon.”",,,"Vickery, Thomas D.

column237: ,"FT Excerpt: Dr. Borden reports a millenial state of things at the Ladies College. There has been so far an almost entire absence of anything like sickness, and in consequence the attendance at classes would probably foot up a fraction  over 99 per cent of the students enrolled. Everything is running smoothly  - the teachers teach with enthusiasm and pleasure and the students love to be taught. Dr. Archibald still continues his labour of love in connection with the college library, having catalogued nearly half the books, which are almost 2,200 in number, with such minute carefullness as to use over 4,000 cards, or about four cards on an average to each volume,   The card cabinet has in fact been found too small and a large one will have to be procured. Some valuable volumes have recently been received from London, and others have been genrously presented by Senator Wood. It is a matter of regret that circumstances render it impossible to alot a single room to the libray, As it is, half the books have to be kept in a little dark room in the Conservatory of Music, near the elevator.",,,"Libraries

column392: ,"FT Excerpt: Dr. W.W.Andrews of Mount Allison, is widely known as a successful and enthusiastic teacher and a pulpit orator of marked originality and eloquence. People in Sackville also know him as a scientific agriculturalist who loveingly studies the "" true inwardness""  of the plant world and tries to find out how his vegetabkes conduct their vital economy, how they are affected by this chemical elemnt or that, or by a change of habitat or surroundings and so on. But the Doctor is also no mean inventor. An ingenious and effective blowpipe  of his invention is manufactured and sold by the Buffalo Dental Company. Max Kachler & Martini, of Berlin, Germany manufacture a superior self-regulating  gas generator invented two or three years ago by Dr. Andrews. And at present he is having made both in the United States and England a versatile piece of apparatus , namely a microscope adapted for general use as a dissecting microscope, a micro-polariscope, a saccharimeter, and, a projection microscope , with a magnifying power of 350 or400 diameters, the whole affair costing less than $25.....",,,"Andrews, Wilbur W.

column687: ,"FT Excerpt: A.M. Faulkner, of Truro, who tunes the pianos and organs of the ladies College and keeps them in repair, met on Thursday night with an accident of a rather singular and very unpleasant nature. Something was wrong with the working of the air-box of the large pipe organ in Beehoven Hall and Mr. Faulkner had gone down into the cellar where the running mechanism of the organ is located, in order to investigate and apply a remedy. He had just got himself well tangled up in the machinery when someone, who did not know what was going on, turned on the water motor and set the whole apparatus in motion, with the result that Mr. Faulkener got quite a bad squeezing and thumping before his cries for help brought Dr. Borden to the rescue and the motor was turned off.",,,"Royal Bank

column157: ,"FT Excerpt""The governor was the Honourable Abner Reid McClelan, who served as an MLA, senator and ultimately as New Brunswick's lieutenant governor from 1896 to 1901, and his beautiful home is now a museum kept alive by the Community Foundation of Southeast New Brunswick."" . . . .. . . . ... . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .""The home was designed by a local architect and fellow Mount Allison graduate, Watson E. Reid, who was Anna McClelan's brother. This was no case of generous nepotism however, as Watson E. Reid was more than up to the task. column144: ,"FT Excerpt: Someone has made the suggestion  - which will probably be wedded to fact ere long, that the ladies College should establish a paper of its own. There can be no doubt that there are college girls at Mount Allison entirely competent to wield the editorial pen, and it is equally certain that in conjunction with the collegiate community there must be an abundance of journalistic material of the most thrilling and interesting nature. Given the ability and the fund of matter, and what more would you have! Let the ""Argosy Folks"" look to their laurels. The Argosy, by the way, is getting on in years, as it is now starting its thirtieth volume. To the Rev. M.R. Knight, Mt. Allison, '75, more than to anyone else is due the credit of its establishment, and chronologically iy probably ranks as a college paper next to the Dalhousie Gazette.",,,"Allison, David

column192: ,"FT: Excerpt   Two recent meetings of the Town Council have been spent discussing the details of the loan which will have to be made in order to pay for the water works. There is an amicable difference of opinion among the members of the council as to the propriety of establishing a sinking fund in connection with the loan, some of the councillors thinking tha it is both in accordance with sound business methods to provide such a fund and that the embodiment of such a proivision in the scheme will facilitate the sale of debentures, others claiming that it is enough for the present generation to have bought the water works, and that the payment for them may just as well be remitted to generations yet to come. ""Why"" they say ""should we do anything for posterity? Posterity has never done anything for us."" The probablilities, however, seem to be that the ""sinking funders"", if they may be so called, will carry the day. On Wednesday there was a heated debate in Council on the question of collecting a tax from people coming into Sackville to engage in business. Some of the councillors seem to have the curious idea that all such persons should be discouraged in every possible way lest there should be any detriment to the business establishments at present existing in Sackville. One can't help wondering what kind of a place Sackville would have been had such a principle been uniformly acted on in the past and who would have stood in the shoes of Mayor Wood, and Councillors Dixon, Copp, Wry and Ryan, for these are all ""aliens and strangers"" in relation to the municipality of Sackville.",,,"Borden, Arthur C.

column498: ,"FT: Extract   Captain Elijah Chase and the steam engline with which he unloads the ships that come to the Port of Sackville are still at work down at the wharf.  Last week he unloaded a cargo of coal and iron for the Maritime Heating Company of Amherst, from the Greta, a vessel to which there attaches a  melancholy interest in this part of the world. Almost exactly a year ago, on a voyage to Sydney, with no cargo and too lightly ballasted for a ship noted as she was for her heavy spars, she capsized, with the loss of Captain McHaffey, of Sackville and all his crew, but she was recovered, repaired, and again sent to sea. Next week Captain Chase expects the arrival of a 500-ton vessel, the Frank G. Walter, of New York, with a cargo of old car wheels for Messrs Rhodes and Curry to be used in casting.",,,"Wharves

column672: ,"FT Excerpt:  The high tide of August 19th tore out the aboideau on Dixon's Creek, about two hundred yards to the south of the ICR station. This aboideau has not yet been replaced, and the proprietors of Mill Creek Body of Marsh. District No. 9 in whose territiory it lies, are not agreed as to whether it should be put in again this autumn or in the spring.  Those who favour the latter course do so, first, because they think they may as well have the benefit through the winter of the fertilizing deposit of mud, which, owing to the close proximity to the head of the Bay accumulates very rapidly on this body of marsh, and secondly, because they claim the work of repair can be effected much more cheaply in the spring than at present An informal meeting of the proprietors was held this week, and it was concluded that it would be adviseable for Marsh Commissioner Sidney Brownell to call a regular, legal meeting for the purpose of considering the problem above. Some of the proprietors are of the opinion that as the ICR line passes through the above mentioned body of marsh, it is legally responsible as a coporate proprietor for its share of the expense to be incurred, in the restoration of the aboideau as well of all similar expenditures in the past, no such dues having ever been collected from it.  A still more important question arises from the fact that the main sewer of the town runs along the eastern side of Lorne Street, passes under the ICR track and empties into the Dixon's Creek alluded to above. Now that the aboideau is gone, whenever the tide rises above a certain level, the town sewage is backed up in the main sewer, the fall of which is exceedingly gentle, and, intermingled with the tidal water, overflows onto the low lying land in the vicinity of the station and even into the Lorne Street ditches. Here is a state of things which evidently calls  for prompt and thorough treatment. The marsh proprietors consider that as their aboideau serves as a vent for the town sewer system, the town aught to share the requisit work of restoration. At a special meeting , however, of the Town Council held Thursday evening for the purpose of considering the situation arising from the tidal elimination of the aboideau, the suggested cooperation with the marsh propreitors was repudiated in toto, the opinion of the councillors being that it was better  for the town to take independent action than to form what might prove to be an embarrassing partnership.",,,"Marshes

column80: ,"Ft: Excerpt   Mr. B.C. Rayworth, carriage manufacturer, etc. Makes and sells to those of his own calling all over the Maritime Province hubs for carriage and cart wheels of all diameters from five inches up. He has just commenced operations in connection with the hubs of next year, for it takes him a year to get them ready for the market. He buys from Westcock, Melrose and other places around about, yellow birch cut in suitable lengths. These blocks he frees from the bark by running them through a lathe, bore through from the axle holes, steams them thoroughly, and lets them season for a year. Then when they are to be put on the market he turns them into shape and bores the spoke holes. This season he will get in about 4000 hub blocks, a thousand more than last year and thinks he could find a ready sale for fifteen or twenty thousand if he had the proper facilities for manufacturing them. This would involve the addition to his plant of a special form of lathe, which could turn out the hubs with incomperably greater speed and economy that is at present possible, and also of a mortising machine, specially devised for this line of manufacture. These improvements, Mr Rayworth will probably have to make ere long, especialy if he extends his sale to England as he possibly may.",,,"Carriage Factories

column122: ,"FT  Excerpt:  The great Missiquash Marsh on the eastern side of the Pointe de Bute ridge which has been in process of reclamation for a number of years past, is this spring, in better condition than ever before. It is anticipated that there will be a grass cut of at least 100 tons - about half being prime timothy and clover and half mixed hay of excellent quality. The returns from this source, moreover, will be largely augmented by the pasturage of a number of cattle and will probably not be devoted to the paying of dividends but to extending and improving the system of ditching upon which depends the success of the entire undertaking. A much greater expenditure in this direction is needed at present than will be case in the future, for, as time goes on , the constant abstraction of water from the soil by drainage causes it to settle and become more solid thus rendering the sides of the ditches very much less liable to crumble and cave in. This marsh, with an area of over 5000 acres, possibly the largest piece in these provinces under the control of a single individual or company, is greatly enhanced in value and rendered more easy to work by the fact that there are no other marsh proprietors in the vicinity to be deferred to or consulted. It practically comprises the entire watershed of the Missiquash as far as marsh land is concerned, and as long as the company conforms to the law, it is absolutely free to adopt whatever methods it pleases in dealing with its property. The Missiquash Marsh is in charge of William Trueman of Prospect Farm, great, great gandson of the original William and the company is fortunate in having secured as manager a person so reliable and so thoroughly versed in marshland lore.",,,"Missiquash Marsh

column162: ,"FT: Excerpt    Our water system has so far not been very largely extended this season. Services, however, have been put into several of the houses that are in process of construction and into the Royal Bank Building. For the inch pipe that supplied this establishment, James Teare, restaurant keeper, baker etc., has had substituted a three inch pipe and will install a water motor, which will make his ice cream, grind his coffee, knead his bread etc. In putting the water into the new woodworking factory down near the ICR station, a six inch pipe has been employed with the view of a possible extension of the system to the houses further along the street.",,,"Water System

column1: 20th of June, 1774
To: Mr. Benjamin Smith
Appleton by Wisk
Near No. 'Allerton.
Yorkshire, Old England.
Dear Brother and Sister,

As I gave an opportunity of sending you a few lines by Christopher Flinloft, without putting you to any expense, and as I could not tell how to let you hear from me sooner and safer I therefore let my former letters bide by me until such times as he returned to Old England and as I had an opportunity I thought proper to let you know a few things that accorded since the date of the other letters.

Its amazing to see and hear the various opinions of the people arrived in Nova Scotia. Some hold the land as good, as the account given by Charles Dixon in his letters to England, others think them pretty good but nothing compared to C. Dixon's account - But a great many, especially the poorest sort, for which I'm greatly concerned, for I cannot see how they are to earn their bread or by what means they must be supported. Those are daily branding C. Dixon with being the author of lies and falsities both to his face and behind his back, and the very gentlemen who have land to sell, and have a desire the Country should be populated as soon as possible, blames Mr.Dixon for his large (economics) upon the Country and its amassing produce.",,,"Metcalf, James

column3: ,"The tremendous tide swept over the whole of the marshes of Cumberland and Westmorland ! When it was considered that almost every farmer adjacent to a tract of (dyked) marsh depends upon it for the principal part of his hay, and that this, after being cut, remains for the most part on the ground in stacks, or in exceptional cases in barns, to be hauled in the winter, the great extent of the losses may be more nearly approximated. But the loss of hay now, great as it is, embraces but a small portion of the damages to the proprietors of (dyked) marshes. The expense of repairing, and in many cases renewing miles of heavy dykes
column4: and the injurious effects upon future grass crops for years, through the action of the tide, to swell up the account of losses ! At half past 10 o'clock on Monday night the dykes overflowed. The water having gradually accumulated on the marshes to the depth of from one to two feet, a wave, similar to the tidal bore, swept up with a rearing noise and a great velocity, carrying almost everything before it
column5: stocks of hay, fences and in many cases, well-filled barns succumbing to its power. Four men who went to Fort Lawrence to secure a schooner sought shelter from the wind in a barn. The tide rising, they abandoned the barn and took to a fence which extended from it to the upland, and by passing along which they hoped to be safe. The waves swept away the fence. Two of the men managed to reach some poles and save themselves. The others were drowned. An old man named Steward, belonging to Minudie, engaged in cutting grass at Minudie Point, was in the habit of spending his nights in a barn there. On the Terrific night, finding the barn afloat and breaking up, he succeeded in clinging to a passing haystack. After being for some time at the mercy of the wind and the tide, hope almost failed as he was being fast borne seaward, when his life-buoy grounded on the top of the dyke, seemingly at the very brink of destruction. He was rescued by means of a boat on the following day. A horse which was in the barn was drowned.
